items.title: Definition of abnormal uterine bleeding
items.contentHtml:
Definition of abnormal uterine bleeding

  • Abnormal uterine bleeding is defined as any bleeding from the uterine corpus that deviates in frequency, duration or volume from the normal menstrual pattern in non-pregnant women.
  • Normal parameters are: cycle of 24 to 38 days, duration of bleeding ≤8 days and volume ≤80 mL per cycle.
  • Bleeding from the lower genital tract (vagina, cervix) and pregnancy are excluded as the primary cause.
  • Acute abnormal uterine bleeding requires urgent evaluation if there is hemodynamic instability or hemoglobin <8 g/dL.

items.title: PALM-COEIN Classification
items.contentHtml:
PALM-COEIN Classification

  • The PALM-COEIN classification (FIGO 2011) divides the causes into structural (PALM) and non-structural (COEIN).
  • P = Endometrial polyp; A = Adenomyosis; L = Leiomyoma (submucosal, intramural, subserous); M = Malignancy/hyperplasia.
  • C = Coagulopathy; O = Ovulatory (ovulatory dysfunction); E = Endometrial (local alteration of hemostasis); I = Iatrogenic (intrauterine device, hormone therapy); N = Not classified.
  • In premenopausal women, the most common structural cause is submucosal leiomyoma; in postmenopausal women, endometrial atrophy and polyp.

items.title: Most frequent structural causes
items.contentHtml:
Most frequent structural causes

  • Submucosal leiomyomas are the most common structural cause of abnormal uterine hemorrhage in reproductive age; They are classified by the FIGO classification (type 0 to 2).
  • Endometrial polyps are focal growths of endometrial glands and stroma; They are usually benign, but the risk of malignancy increases with age (>45 years).
  • Adenomyosis is characterized by the presence of endometrial glands in the myometrium; It is associated with pelvic pain and heavy bleeding.
  • Endometrial hyperplasia without atypia has a low risk of cancer (<5%); with atypia, the risk of associated endometrial cancer is 25-30%.

items.title: Initial evaluation with ultrasound
items.contentHtml:
Initial evaluation with ultrasound

  • Transvaginal ultrasound is the first-line test to evaluate abnormal uterine bleeding; measures endometrial thickness and detects masses.
  • In premenopausal women, an endometrial thickness >16 mm (in secretory phase) or >10 mm (in follicular phase) requires additional evaluation.
  • In postmenopausal women, the threshold for indicating biopsy is >4 mm (99% negative predictive value for endometrial cancer if <4 mm).
  • Hysterosonography (saline infusion) improves the detection of submucosal polyps and leiomyomas, with sensitivity >90%.

items.title: Endometrium in postmenopausal women
items.contentHtml:
Endometrium in postmenopausal women

  • In all postmenopausal women with abnormal uterine bleeding, the endometrial thickness by transvaginal ultrasound should be ≤4 mm to be considered normal.
  • A thickness >4 mm requires endometrial biopsy (preferably by hysteroscopy) to rule out hyperplasia with atypia or endometrial cancer.
  • In asymptomatic postmenopausal women, the incidental finding of endometrium >4 mm does not require biopsy if there is no bleeding.
  • Endometrial atrophy is the most common cause of postmenopausal bleeding, with thin endometrium (<4 mm) and without atypia.

items.title: Treatment with NSAIDs
items.contentHtml:
Treatment with NSAIDs

  • Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are the first line of pharmacology to reduce heavy menstrual bleeding, acting as cyclooxygenase inhibitors and decreasing the synthesis of vasodilatory prostaglandins.
  • Mefenamic acid at a dose of 500 mg every 8 hours during bleeding days reduces menstrual flow by 20-50%.
  • Naproxen (500 mg initially, then 250 mg every 6-8 hours) is also effective, with similar evidence to mefenamic acid.
  • NSAIDs are more effective in abnormal uterine bleeding due to ovulatory dysfunction than in bleeding due to structural causes; They should not be used if there is coagulopathy or active peptic ulcer.

items.title: Levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine device
items.contentHtml:
Levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine device

  • The levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine device (LNG-IUD, 52 mg) is the first-line medical treatment for abnormal uterine bleeding without a treatable structural cause, reducing bleeding by 80-90% at 6 months.
  • It acts by atrophy of the endometrium and thickening of cervical mucus; Its effectiveness is comparable to endometrial ablation.
  • It is associated with irregular bleeding in the first 3-6 months; then it produces amenorrhea in 50% of users per year.
  • Contraindicated in suspected endometrial cancer, active pelvic inflammatory disease or uterine malformations that distort the cavity.

items.title: Endometrial ablation
items.contentHtml:
Endometrial ablation

  • Endometrial ablation is a minimally invasive surgical procedure that destroys the entire thickness of the endometrium, indicated in abnormal uterine bleeding refractory to medical treatment and without desire for future fertility.
  • The most used techniques include thermal balloon ablation, radiofrequency and cryotherapy; the success rate (reduction of bleeding to normal or amenorrhea) is 80-90%.
  • Absolute contraindications: current or desired pregnancy, endometrial malignancy, distorted uterine cavity (large submucosal leiomyomas type 2-3) or active infection.
  • Does not prevent residual endometrial hyperplasia; Contraception is required if the uterus remains intact, and is not effective if there is profound symptomatic adenomyosis.

items.title: Endometrial hyperplasia and cancer risk
items.contentHtml:
Endometrial hyperplasia and cancer risk

  • Endometrial hyperplasia is classified histologically as without atypia or with atypia; the latter is considered a direct precursor to endometrial cancer type I.
  • The risk of progression to endometrial cancer in hyperplasia without atypia is <5% in 20 years; in hyperplasia with atypia, the risk is 25-30% if untreated.
  • The first-line treatment for hyperplasia without atypia is oral progestins or levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine device; for hyperplasia with atypia, total hysterectomy is recommended.
  • In women desiring fertility with hyperplasia with atypia, conservative treatment with high-dose progestogens (megestrol acetate 160 mg/day) and monitoring every 3 months may be considered.

items.title: Anovulatory dysfunctional uterine bleeding
items.contentHtml:
Anovulatory dysfunctional uterine bleeding

  • Anovulatory dysfunctional uterine bleeding is an abnormal uterine hemorrhage without an organic cause, due to chronic anovulation and endometrial exposure to estrogens without progesterone.
  • It is more common in adolescents and perimenopausal women; It is characterized by unpredictable bleeding, often abundant and prolonged.
  • The diagnosis is one of exclusion after ruling out pregnancy, structural causes and coagulopathies; Endometrial biopsy is indicated if there are risk factors (age >45 years, obesity, polycystic ovary syndrome).
  • First-line treatment is cyclical progesterone (medroxyprogesterone acetate 10 mg/day for 12-14 days per month) or levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine device if there is no desire for fertility.

items.title: Differential diagnosis with pregnancy
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis with pregnancy

  • In all women of reproductive age with abnormal uterine bleeding, pregnancy should be ruled out by serum or urine human chorionic gonadotropin testing before any diagnostic or therapeutic intervention.
  • Obstetric causes of bleeding include: threatened abortion, ongoing abortion, ectopic pregnancy (especially if there is adnexal pain), and trophoblastic disease gestational.
  • Slight bleeding with a positive pregnancy test suggests incipient abortion or ectopic pregnancy; Transvaginal ultrasound evaluates location and vitality.
  • Ectopic pregnancy presents with pelvic pain and irregular bleeding; ultrasound may show adnexal mass and absence of intrauterine sac; Treatment is surgical or with methotrexate if it is early and without rupture.

items.title: Emergency management with intravenous estrogens
items.contentHtml:
Emergency management with intravenous estrogens

  • In severe acute abnormal uterine bleeding with hemodynamic instability, the first measure is fluid resuscitation and transfusions if necessary.
  • Intravenous conjugated estrogens at doses of 25 mg every 4-6 hours (maximum 6 doses) are the first-line pharmacological treatment to stop acute bleeding, promoting endometrial regeneration.
  • If an intravenous route is not available, oral ethinyl estradiol can be used at a dose of 0.05 mg every 6 hours or high-dose combined oral contraceptives (up to 4 tablets per day).
  • Once the bleeding is controlled (usually in 24-48 hours), cyclical progestogens or an intrauterine device should be continued. levonorgestrel releaserto prevent recurrence; If unresponsive, consider intrauterine balloon tamponade or arterial embolization.
